speaker: interviewer
text: Our survivor is Pearl Sapoznik and today is December 3, 1996. Do you ever have any dreams or nightmares as a result of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I have, yeah. Just before the-- and that's-- that's-- that's a true story. Just before the war broke out, I had a dream about the old-- the-- the-- the war. Everything, with the planes, with the bombarding, everything, but it was like [SPEAKING GERMAN], it's like-- but you know, it's all just-- just you know, what you do. I didn't even I didn't even repeat. It was- that was this, but then, being in Russia, I have constant-- constant dreams about my sister. There was constantly dreams about my sister, about my friend that I mentioned before, Silver Streindell. She was a pretty girl. I had a-- a lot of dreams about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now? Do you have any now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? No, not now. Not so. Not so. But right-- right during the war, I had a lot of dreams, bad dreams, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back to the war, was there and one thought form that comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it shouldn't happened. They shouldn't kill so many people. That's all. Just because they're Jewish, you have to kill them? They didn't bother anybody. They mind their own business. They believe in their own god, so why do you have to kill them? Children? Yeah, shouldn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any advice for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell them they should remember always the way we-- the way we-- what we went through, that they should never forget this. And they know. We talk very-- even my husband used to talk. The children, they know everything that we went through, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they react when they heard this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my middle granddaughter, she went back to-- the living-- the living-- living--

speaker: interviewer
text: The March of the Living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the March of the Living. She went there, but I have even the album. She gave me the album. She wanted to see it-- a lot of pictures. So they went to the Holocaust Museum. Then they went to Poland. They went to Majdanek, to Auschwitz, and to all of Krakow. Then they went to Israel for the Yom Ha'atzmaut. And now, Steven, my grandson, he's also-- he belongs to B'nai B'rith. He also wants to go, yeah. They know. They know everything. They-- Yeshiva children, you know, they-- they know. They know a lot about Yiddishkeit, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of Jewish education were you able to supply for your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my children-- my Norman, they all went to Yeshiva. Norman went to Chaim Berlin Yeshiva. Henry went to the Laboratory Yeshiva. So I gave them beside, English, and then they-- they start, you know, college. Yeah, but they-- they had good-- good-- good Yiddish-- Yiddish education, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you now like to deliver to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now that I lived already my life-- I mean, a message or just for-- for myself? I want to just be all right. I shouldn't be a burden on my children. I should have my mind and I should be able to do what I like to do. With that, I'll be very thankful to God, yeah. To have my friends-- I have a lot of friends, good friends, you know, distant friend, but friends, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. Thank you. OK, this picture was taken on a bris. My father's youngest brother, Benyamin, his wife just gave birth to a boy, [? Triengula. ?] And on the-- on to my left is my father's older brother, Shlema and next to him is my father's oldest brother, Shmiel and then is Benyamin, and then is my father, and-- in-- next to my father is my father's oldest daughter, Ruchel. And in front of Shlema is his wife, it was name was Surke, and she's holding a baby. In between them is a-- a little boy named Bena. In front of my father's oldest brother, his wife is named Mala. And in front of Benyamin is his wife, whose name was Chaiperl. And in front of my father is my mother, Rivke. And those are the children of-- Benjamin's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Surke is holding a little girl is named [? Hasia ?] and the boy near Shlema, his name is [? Bentia. ?] And in front of Mala is-- is Benyamin's younger daughter, is name-- her name was Tsilla, [INAUDIBLE]. And Chaiperl is holding, I think his name was [? Hasia ?] also. And near my mother sits Benyamin's older daughter, Luba, and the little boy's named is [? Henach ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken probably it was about 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Benjamin's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived not far from us in Rovno. OK, this picture, this is my mother, my father, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us their full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Rivke, my father, Isaac, and this is-- this-- this is me, Pearl. I was probably not even a year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Rovno. Was taken, yeah. This picture was taken in Rovno on the bridge-- on a bridge. My-- my sister Ruchel, my little sister Pesie, and myself. This was taken in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steinberg. We were all Steinbergs at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this bridge? What did it cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bridge was crossed right through the city, in the middle of the city. Yeah, it was a nice-- nice bridge, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it over a river?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a river, yeah, the Ustya, [NON-ENGLISH] Ustya, right over-- over a river. Yeah, that's what I said. It was taken in 1936. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, this picture was taken on the cemetery, on my grandmother's grave. I-- I was named after my grandmother, Pearl. My father's brother came from America, and the first thing I did is-- is fix the-- the-- the matzevahs. So this is my-- my-- to your left is my father's sister, [? Enya. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Enya ?] [? Gunya. ?] And this is my father's brother, Duvid Steinberg, and to your right, it my father's sister, [? Enya, ?] and my father's-- [? Enya ?] [? Loynau ?] and my father's oldest brother, Shmiel Steinberg, and they fixed the-- the-- the matzevahs. Did I mention the year when it was taken? In 1932, probably. Yeah, 1932. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This of my-- this is my wedding picture. I didn't have exactly a wedding gown. My husband and myself. This was taken in 1945 in Rovno. I got married in Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zindel, yeah. Zindel Sapoznik. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my husband, [INAUDIBLE] Zindel Sapoznik. He was always going with Simon Wiesenthal. Whatever they have a memorial, they used to go. Used to take him all over these places, to different camps. This is one of them, a memorial after the perished people, what that perished in the concentration camps. Always going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we see in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see my husband is holding the [INAUDIBLE]. And this is-- to your left is Mr. Simon Wiesenthal. And the others, I don't remember the names. They were all like, you know, you know, like presidents of some kind of society, but that was-- that is-- Simon Wiesenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was taken in, I think it was in [NON-ENGLISH], yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my mother and my father. This picture was taken from Henry's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was that? 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents' names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Isaac Steinberg, my mother, Rivke Steinberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, this is a picture of my older son, Norman Sapoznik, and his wife, Yaffa Sapoznik, and they're holding Steven [NON-ENGLISH] Sapoznik.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken when Steven was just born. It was 1982. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, this is a picture that was our 40th wedding anniversary. This is my husband, Zindel. Next to him is my oldest granddaughter, Dalia Rivke. Next to her is her mother, my daughter-in-law, Yaffa. And next to Yaffa is my middle granddaughter, Michelle. And next to Michelle, that's me and I'm holding my younger son. I mean I'm holding him-- I forgot to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Sapoznik. And in front of us is Steven Sapoznik. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Chaim [INAUDIBLE], Henry Sapoznik, my younger son. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Growing up in this family, my late father and my mother and my-- my grandparents and the Sapoznik-Steinberg family gave me an unparalleled view, not into the destruction of the Yiddish civilization of Eastern Europe, but into the powerful continuity and cultural consciousness of my family. As much as we talked about the experiences of the Holocaust as a sort of a daily reminder of who we were, my family also championed the literacy and joy of the language and the culture. For us, it was never a sepia-toned, nostalgic view. It was always a very strong and common part of our everyday lives, a firm foundation of the lives that they had in Eastern Europe, a very strong bridge into the life that they brought with them here, and happily for myself, a matrix for being able to construct something as I did, my interest--- my personal interest in klezmer music, which has now become a worldwide phenomenon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for myself, it was a personal mission and the resonance for hundreds or thousands of other people who now take great pleasure in the renaissance of Yiddish culture. From this family, a gift to everyone in America and in the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank, you, Hen. you, Mom.
